p.strong: Very Long Baseline Interferometry with the SKA

ac:rich-text-body.p: VLBI combines radio signals from widely separated telescopes to synthesize an extremely large virtual antenna, achieving angular resolutions far beyond individual telescopes. SKA-Mid will provide transformative VLBI capability, enabling unprecedented studies of compact objects and fine structure in galaxies.

p: Very Long Baseline Interferometry is a technique in which signals from geographically separated radio telescopes are recorded and later cross-correlated to produce images with angular resolution determined by the longest baseline rather than the individual telescope sizes.

Key principle: An interferometer's angular resolution is θ ~ λ / D, where D is the baseline length. By using baselines spanning continents or planet Earth, VLBI achieves milliarcsecond to micro-arcsecond resolutions.

ul.li: Milliarcsecond resolution unattainable by individual telescopes
Direct measurement of compact source structure (jets, cores, accretion disks)
Astrometry with micro-arcsecond precision
Ability to "image the invisible"—detecting and resolving sources hidden in dust or obscured by confusion
Frequency range: 0.35–15.4 GHz (continuous coverage)
Array size and sensitivity: 197 dishes provide unprecedented sensitivity when combined with global VLBI networks
Data rate: High-bandwidth capability for correlation with international partners
Technology: Full VLBI capability (recording, real-time transmission, or playback correlation)

p: SKA-Low VLBI: Limited capability at low frequencies; best used for specific science cases requiring low-frequency resolution (e.g., 110 MHz VLBI). Most VLBI observations will use SKA-Mid.
SKA-Mid will integrate with established global VLBI networks:

ul.li: Network of ~15 radio telescopes across Europe, Africa, and Asia
Routine observations at 1.6–22 GHz
SKA-Mid participation will extend sensitivity and baseline coverage toward southern hemisphere
Australian and southern-hemisphere network
Observations at 1.4–8.4 GHz
SKA-Mid, co-located in southern Africa, will strengthen baseline distributions
Dedicated 10-element US array with baselines to 8,600 km
Operates at multiple frequencies (1.6–43 GHz)
SKA-Mid can be included in VLBA observing sessions via electronic transmission
Ad-hoc arrays combining telescopes from multiple networks and continents
SKA-Mid's sensitivity will enable fainter source detection in global arrays
High data rates allow real-time participation or rapid playback correlation

p: VLBI observations impose specific technical requirements:

ul.li: Data rate: Typical VLBI observations: 512 Mbps to several Gbps per telescope
Network requirements: High-capacity fiber-optic links connecting SKA-Mid to correlation centers (EVN, VLBA)
Latency: Minimal latency (<1 second) for real-time processing
Advantage: Results available within days of observation
Data shipping: Recorded data is shipped (via disk drives, tape, or high-capacity transfers) to central correlation facility
Correlation facility: Specialized computing (e.g., EVN MkIV or VLBA hardware correlator) cross-correlates all baselines
Timeline: Results available 1–4 weeks post-observation (depending on data volume and correlator queue)
Storage: Playback mode requires on-site recording infrastructure at SKA-Mid
SKA-Mid can transmit electronic data while co-observing telescopes record to disk, later correlating all baselines together
Enables most flexible use of global VLBI resources
Jet structure: Resolve jets on parsec and sub-parsec scales near black hole event horizons
Hotspot morphology: Study compact radio lobes and regions of particle acceleration
Core brightness temperature: Diagnose magnetic field strength and plasma conditions
Variability: Monitor rapid flux and structural changes in AGN cores
Pulsars: Resolve interstellar scattering and measure precise positions
X-ray binaries: Pinpoint positions and track orbital motion at high precision
Stellar masers: Resolve maser emission regions in evolved stars and star-forming regions
Parallax measurement: Micro-arcsecond astrometry for accurate distances to nearby galaxies and calibration of cosmic distance ladder
Proper motion: Track motion of compact sources over years for orbital mass determinations
Proper motion studies: Measure galactic rotation and dynamics in external galaxies
Lensed AGN: Resolve multiple images and lens geometry at milliarcsecond scales
Time delays: Measure gravitational time delays for Hubble constant determination (cosmology)
OH masers: Map maser spatial distribution in AGN narrow-line regions and disks
H2O masers: Resolve accretion disk geometry and black hole mass in nearby galaxies
SiO masers: Resolve circumstellar maser emission in AGB stars
GRB localizations: Provide precise positions for rapid follow-up by other facilities
Neutron star mergers: Localize kilonovae and constrain jet physics
VLBI ToO (Target-of-Opportunity): Rapid VLBI observations of unexpected transient events

tr.td: Image noise ~10–50 µJy/beam (EVN; depends on array and frequency)

p: VLBI proposals are typically submitted to the host network's Call for Proposals:

ul.li: EVN observations: Apply to the European VLBI Network Steering Committee (3 calls per year)
VLBA observations: Apply to the NRAO (National Radio Astronomy Observatory) Proposal System
Global arrays: Coordinate proposals across multiple networks

p: When submitting a VLBI proposal involving SKA-Mid, include:

ul.li: Scientific justification: Why milliarcsecond or micro-arcsecond resolution is essential
Target list: Source names, coordinates, expected brightness, and spectral index
Frequency selection: Required frequency band(s) and rationale
Array configuration: Desired baseline distribution and international partners
Sensitivity requirements: Expected image rms noise and time on source
Data handling: Data transmission method (real-time or playback) and correlation site
Feasibility statement: Confirmation of compatibility with SKA-Mid VLBI capabilities (check with SKA User Support)
SKA operations: Scheduling and array configuration
International VLBI networks: Network scheduling and data transport
Correlation facility: Data correlation and imaging
Science teams: Target selection and calibration strategy

p: Early contact with the SKA User Support team is recommended when planning VLBI proposals to ensure feasibility and coordination.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Full VLBI capability (real-time electronic transmission and playback recording) is expected during operational cycle 2 onwards. Early-cycle VLBI observations may be limited in scope or available only through collaborative arrangements with specific networks. Check the SKA Cycle Announcement for current VLBI capabilities.

ul.li: SKA VLBI Science Book: Comprehensive overview of SKA VLBI science cases and technical specifications
EVN User Guide: Guidance for submitting proposals to the European VLBI Network (www.evlbi.org)
VLBA User Guide: Information on VLBA observations and the proposal system (www.vlba.nrao.edu)
SKA User Support: Contact for coordination and feasibility advice on VLBI proposals

ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA VLBI community is active and collaborative. Joining the SKA VLBI Science Working Group is an excellent way to stay informed about opportunities and connect with other VLBI researchers planning observations with the SKA.
